title: Boolean Algebra
summary: Master the algebra of 0s and 1s — the foundation of every digital circuit.

sections.heading: Why Boolean Algebra Matters
sections.body: Digital circuits operate on two discrete voltage levels, abstracted as 0 (LOW) and 1 (HIGH). Boolean algebra gives us a compact mathematical language to describe, simplify, and prove properties of logic networks before we ever draw a gate.
bullets: Every combinational circuit is a Boolean function
Simplification reduces gate count, power, and delay
Algebraic manipulation is the basis of logic synthesis tools

sections.heading: Basic Operators
sections.body: The three fundamental operations mirror logic gates: AND (·), OR (+), and NOT (¯). XOR (⊕) is also common in arithmetic and parity circuits.
bullets: AND: A · B = 1 only when both A and B are 1
OR: A + B = 1 when at least one input is 1
XOR: A ⊕ B = 1 when inputs differ

callout.text: In Verilog, AND is &, OR is |, NOT is ~, and XOR is ^. Multi-bit operations use bitwise versions.

sections.heading: Key Identities and Laws
sections.body: Boolean algebra obeys identities similar to ordinary algebra, but with important differences — there is no subtraction or division, and everything is modulo 2.
bullets: Idempotent: A + A = A, A · A = A
De Morgan: (A + B)̄ = Ā · B̄ and (A · B)̄ = Ā + B̄

sections.heading: SOP and POS Forms
sections.body: Sum-of-Products (SOP) expresses a function as OR of AND terms (minterms). Product-of-Sums (POS) expresses it as AND of OR terms (maxterms). Karnaugh maps and Quine-McCluskey use these canonical forms.
bullets: Minterm: product term that is 1 for exactly one input combination
Maxterm: sum term that is 0 for exactly one input combination
Any Boolean function can be written in SOP or POS form

callout.text: When simplifying by hand, convert to SOP first, then group adjacent 1s on a K-map.

sections.heading: Duality Principle
sections.body: Every Boolean identity remains valid if we swap AND with OR and 0 with 1. This duality halves the number of laws you need to memorize and explains the symmetry between NAND/NOR universality.

quiz.question: What is the result of A · Ā for any value of A?
quiz.explanation: A · Ā is the complement law: a variable ANDed with its inverse is always 0.

quiz.question: Apply De Morgan's law to (X + Y)̄:
quiz.explanation: De Morgan: the complement of a sum equals the product of complements.

quiz.question: Which expression is in Sum-of-Products (SOP) form?
quiz.explanation: SOP is an OR of product (AND) terms. AB + CD fits this pattern.

quiz.question: Simplify: A + A · B
quiz.explanation: Absorption law: A + A·B = A. When A=1 the expression is 1; when A=0, A·B=0 so result is A.

quiz.question: What does the idempotent law state for OR?
quiz.explanation: Idempotent: A OR A equals A — duplicating an input does not change the output.

quiz.question: If F = A ⊕ B, when is F = 1?
quiz.explanation: XOR outputs 1 when inputs are different — exclusive OR.

flashcards.back: Ā · B̄ — complement of OR becomes AND of complements.
A product term that is 1 for exactly one row of the truth table.

flashcards.front: SOP vs POS — which uses OR of ANDs?
